ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to iced cupcake exists; A00GG (Cakes and pastries) used as the closest defensible parent category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cupcake with icing; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cupcake with icing weighs approximately 75 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as sold in bakeries and supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Cupcake with icing — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Cupcake with icing' (cr_id F02010010). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrients not reported by HPB for this entry; saturated fat and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for white cake with frosting (NDB 18101) as the closest approved-source analogue, documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Cake and Baked Goods — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for plain iced cupcake based on vanilla cake with icing (GI ~67)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for 'cupcake with icing' found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 (medium) is estimated from the closest analogous entry: vanilla sponge cake with icing, as published in the University of Sydney GI Database and consistent with Foster-Powell et al. (2002) International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 67 × (55.9 × 0.75) / 100 = 67 × 41.925 / 100 = 28.1 per 75 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (36.5 g/100g) with low fibre (1.1 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 370 mg/100g is borderline elevated and potassium is low (90 mg/100g), providing no offsetting vasodilatory benefit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid regular consumption of iced cupcakes; the combination of high refined sugar, moderate sodium, and negligible potassium promotes fluid retention and sustained blood pressure elevation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 36.5 g/100g far exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling; total carbohydrate is 55.9 g/100g with only 1.1 g/100g dietary fibre, producing rapid and severe postprandial blood glucose spikes; GI of 67 (medium) with high carbohydrate density yields a glycaemic load of ~28 per 75 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit iced cupcakes; even a single 75 g serving delivers approximately 42 g of carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of ~28, which will cause clinically significant postprandial hyperglycaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g) exceeds the 5 g/100g threshold for cardiovascular concern; trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) from hydrogenated shortening in icing raises LDL and lowers HDL cholesterol; high refined sugar promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid iced cupcakes due to the combined burden of saturated fat, trans fat, and high refined sugar, all of which independently and synergistically worsen lipid profiles and promote atherosclerotic progression.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g) exceeds the 5 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold; trans fat (~0.2 g/100g) is directly pro-inflammatory; refined sugar (36.5 g/100g) promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine pathways.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid iced cupcakes; the combination of trans fat, saturated fat, and high refined sugar promotes systemic inflammation and may exacerbate joint symptoms and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A cupcake with icing is a small individual-portion baked cake made from a standard sponge or butter cake batter — typically containing wheat flour, sugar, butter or vegetable shortening, eggs, and milk — baked in a paper or foil cup and topped with a layer of sweet icing or frosting, usually made from icing sugar, butter, and flavouring. The texture is soft and moist with a tender crumb, and the icing adds a sweet, smooth or slightly firm coating. Cupcakes with icing are popular as desserts, party foods, and celebration treats across Western and Westernised food cultures, including Singapore bakeries and cafes. Nutritionally, this food is energy-dense at 372 kcal per 100 g, with high total carbohydrate (55.9 g/100g), very high sugar (36.5 g/100g), moderate fat (14.9 g/100g) including approximately 8.5 g saturated fat and trace trans fat from shortening-based icing, and moderate sodium (370 mg/100g). Dietary fibre is low at 1.1 g/100g. The glycaemic index is approximately 67 (medium), but the high carbohydrate density produces a glycaemic load of approximately 28 per 75 g serving. This food is clinically unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sugar, saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium content. It is best treated as an occasional indulgence rather than a regular dietary item.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a cupcake with icing?
Is iced cupcake suitable for hypertension?
How much sugar is in a cupcake with icing?
Is frosted cupcake high in fat?
Can I eat cupcake if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02010010, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy closest analogue (NDB 18101) and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for vanilla sponge cake with icing and clearly flagged as analogous_estimate.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported by HPB Singapore Foodo for this entry, and not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for the closest analogue at the required specificity. 2. cholesterol_mg set to -1: not reported by HPB for this entry. 3. All vitamin and mineral fields not reported by HPB (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) set to -1: data not available in HPB Foodo for this entry as of 2026-04-16. 4. saturated_fat_g (~8.5 g/100g) and trans_fat_g (~0.2 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for white cake with frosting (NDB 18101) as the closest approved-source analogue; these are approximate values and should be updated if HPB publishes detailed fatty acid data for this entry. 5. potassium_mg (90 mg/100g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogue; flagged as approximate. 6. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (vanilla sponge cake with icing); no direct cupcake-with-icing GI entry exists in the database. 7. Glycemic load per serving (75 g): GL = 67 × (55.9 × 0.75) / 100 = 28.1. 8. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. Local names fields are null by design. 9. Halal status marked true as a general default for plain baked goods; verification against specific product formulation is recommended if used in a halal-certified context. 10. ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the G5 hard gate validator. This was the sole cause of the REJECT decision in iteration 2. All other sections preserved from iteration 2 draft.
Iteration 3: Corrected glycemic_load_per_serving from 25.1 to 28.1 to accurately reflect the calculation GL = 67 × (55.9 × 0.75) / 100 = 28.1 per 75 g serving.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
